entry.title: Re: SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud | Business Roles for User Ma...
entry.content:
Hello @quocdt5 

Yes, creating two separate roles is a good and recommended approach,

User Management Role-
Maintain business users
Lock/unlock users
Assign/remove business roles
Use IAM apps related to Business User Management

Role Management Role-
Create and maintain business roles
Maintain restrictions
Manage business catalogs
Use IAM apps related to Business Role Management


Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: Accelerate Business Transformation with SAP Activate and the Agent-led Toolchain
entry.content:
Successful business transformation requires the right methodology, the right tools, and the right skills. The BTM live session programme for H2 2026 brings together SAP Activate fundamentals, workstream deep dives, certification preparation, and intensive hands-on bootcamps — all designed to help consultants, project managers, and transformation leads deliver faster, more predictable outcomes.

The hands-on bootcamps place a particular focus on the agent-led toolchain — the integrated use of SAP Signavio, SAP LeanIX, and WalkMe alongside SAP Activate — for both new business transformation programmes and SAP S/4HANA system conversions.

What You Will Learn

  • Core SAP Activate methodology — phases, workstreams, and key deliverables.
  • How to apply the agent-led toolchain across the full transformation lifecycle.
  • Certification preparation for SAP Activate Project Manager and S/4HANA Project Systems.
  • Practical bootcamp exercises covering both business transformation and system conversion scenarios.

entry.title: AI-Driven SAP Performance Insights
entry.content:
All my fellow SAP Basis admins know how useful the HANA Expensive Statements view in DBACOCKPIT can be.

It gives us detailed insight into performance-critical information like execution duration, CPU usage, memory consumption, application source, work process details, and much more. It is one of those views we often rely on when troubleshooting database performance issues.

But there is a challenge we all run into: the data does not stay around for long.

In a typical SAP S/4HANA system, expensive statement history is usually short-lived — often only around 6–8 hours in practical scenarios . So if an issue is reported later in the day, the next morning, or after a month-end process, the most useful evidence may already be gone.

That raises important questions:

 

  • When did the issue actually start?
  • How did the query perform yesterday or during month-end close?
  • Which users executed it in the past month?
  • What parameters made it expensive compared with normal executions?

 

With everything AI can do today, I started thinking: why not use AI to overcome the limitation of short-lived HANA expensive statement data — and make the analysis easier for both technical and functional teams?

That idea led to the HANA Expensive Statement Analyzer, an application designed to convert short-lived HANA expensive statement data into historical, searchable, natural-language insights

Solution Overview

The idea behind this solution is simple: capture the expensive statement data before it disappears, store it historically, and then use AI to make that data easier to analyze.

At a high level, the solution is built in three layers:

Layer 1: Data Collection and Persistence

An ABAP program periodically reads data from the HANA expensive statements source i.e. M_EXPENSIVE_STATEMENTS, and stores the relevant details in a custom SAP table.

A CDS view is then built on top of this table to structure and expose the historical data in a reusable way. This creates the foundation for analyzing expensive statements beyond the short retention window normally available in DBACOCKPIT.

Layer 2: API Exposure

The CDS-based data model is exposed through OData and made available as an API using SAP BTP AppRouter.

This layer helps securely expose the historical expensive statement data so it can be consumed by downstream applications or AI-based tools.

Layer 3: AI-Powered Natural Language Analysis

The API is then connected to an enterprise AI assistant, with a system prompt designed to help users ask meaningful performance-related questions in natural language.

For example, instead of manually filtering through historical records, users can ask questions like:

 

  • What were the top expensive statements today?
  • When did this query first become expensive?
  • Which time window had the highest database load last week?
  • Compare normal versus expensive executions for this statement.
  • Were there any expensive queries during month-end close?
  • Summarize performance issues for the last 30 days in business terms.

 

This makes the solution useful not only for SAP Basis administrators, but also for SAP developers and functional teams. Administrators can troubleshoot using current and historical data, developers can review expensive queries without recreating the issue, and business teams can ask performance questions in plain language.

A Look at the Tool in Action

Let’s take a quick look at how the tool works and how it can be useful for different personas — SAP Administrators, Developers, and Functional teams.

Below are a few sample prompts that show how each persona can use the tool in day-to-day performance analysis.

SAP Administrators

In the example below, I asked the tool to list the top 5 SQL statements by memory consumption for the day.

Along with returning the top 5 statements, the tool also provides useful observations such as:

 

  • Which statements consumed the most memory
  • Whether the statements belong to the same functional area
  • Whether the same statement hash appeared multiple times
  • Which user or application pattern may need further investigation
  • Whether the issue looks like a repeated expensive query pattern

 

This gives SAP Administrators a quick starting point for troubleshooting. Instead of only looking at raw expensive statement records, they can immediately see summarized insights that help identify users, execution times, application sources, peak load windows, and possible patterns for deeper analysis .

Sample prompt:

What are the top 5 SQLs by memory todayrsonal Favorite: Finding Busy and Idle Windows

 

One of my favorite examples is asking the tool to analyze a longer period of performance data and identify system usage trends.

Instead of looking at one expensive statement at a time, I can ask a broader question like:

Based on performance data for July, considering both memory and CPU, can you build a trend of the busiest times and idle times in the system?

This is where the historical data becomes really powerful. Since the expensive statement data is captured and stored over time, the tool can analyze patterns across days, weeks, or business cycles instead of being limited to the short-lived DBACOCKPIT history.

These are just a couple of examples, but the opportunities are endless. By combining historical expensive statement data with AI-powered natural-language analysis, SAP Administrators can quickly identify patterns, peak load windows, recurring expensive queries, and areas that need deeper investigation

SAP Developers

SAP Developers can use the tool to quickly identify expensive SQL statements originating from custom programs, CDS views, or application sources. Instead of waiting to recreate the issue or collect new traces, developers can analyze historical executions, compare expensive versus normal runs, and identify recurring patterns that may point to optimization opportunities.

For example, a developer can ask:

Can you list the top 5 memory-consuming statements for June 2026 originating from Z/custom application sources?

The tool can then summarize the most expensive custom/Z-originated statements, highlight memory and CPU usage, show repeated statement hashes, and provide key observations that help developers decide where to focus tuning efforts.

This helps developers move faster from “which query is expensive?” to “what custom object or pattern should I investigate and optimize?

 

SAP Functional Teams

Functional teams can use the tool as a self-service capability to get quick insights into expensive queries related to their business areas. Instead of waiting for detailed technical analysis, they can ask performance questions in natural language and understand whether a performance issue may be related to user selections, business process volume, reporting filters, or other functional factors within their purview .

By reviewing the parameters passed to a query, functional teams can often identify whether the issue is caused by broad selections, missing filters, large date ranges, high-volume business scenarios, or unusual usage patterns. This helps them validate the business context before engaging SAP Administrators or Developers for deeper technical troubleshooting.

For example, a functional user can ask:

Based on July performance data, list the top 5 expensive queries by memory in the finance area.

The tool can summarize the most expensive queries for that business area, highlight related objects, and provide tuning-focused observations that can help functional teams collaborate more effectively with Basis and development teams.

This makes performance analysis more accessible beyond technical teams and helps move troubleshooting from “technical data only” to a shared conversation between functional, Basis, and development teams.

 

Conclusion

AI gives SAP Administrators a powerful opportunity to simplify routine analysis, reduce manual effort, and focus more on complex performance challenges. By combining historical HANA expensive statement data with natural-language analysis, we can move beyond short-lived monitoring and turn technical performance data into actionable insights.

This helps SAP Administrators, Developers, and Functional teams troubleshoot faster, identify recurring patterns, and collaborate more effectively when resolving performance issues.

Using AI in the right way allows us to add more value to the business — solving complex problems with better context, faster turnaround time, and more meaningful insights.

Please share your feedback, and feel free to comment if you have any technical questions about the solution. I would be happy to exchange ideas and learn from others working on similar SAP performance challenges.

entry.title: Governing the End-to-End Service Cycle in SAP S/4HANA Service with SAP Joule Agents
entry.content:
Introduction

Service is one of the most demanding business processes in SAP S/4HANA.

Every customer interaction can turn into a Service Request, a Service Quotation, a Service Order, and eventually a Service Confirmation — each with its own mandatory data, its own upstream dependency, and its own point at which mistakes become expensive.

Even with modern Fiori experiences, service teams still spend a large part of their day navigating between apps, resolving Business Partners, checking statuses, and validating whether upstream documents are actually ready. When something is missed, the cost is not just rework — it is billing errors, compliance gaps, and eroded customer trust.

With the arrival of SAP Joule, SAP Joule Studio, and the Generative AI Hub in SAP AI Core, we now have a governed way to bring intelligent, conversational experiences directly into these processes — without stepping outside SAP's business rules.

This blog walks through three tightly integrated SAP Joule Agents that together govern the complete service cycle in SAP S/4HANA — a Service Intake Assistant, a Service Order Assistant, and a Service Confirmation Assistant — and shares the design principles and lessons learned from building them.

If you are new to SAP Joule Studio or the Generative AI Hub, the SAP resources linked at the end of this blog are a good place to start before diving in.

The Business Challenge:

Service organizations across Industrial Equipment, Engineering Services, Field Service, and Utilities consistently report the same pain points across the service cycle:

  • Manual navigation and fragmented validation during intake
  • Ambiguous Business Partner resolution leading to downstream billing issues
  • Service Orders created without valid upstream commercial references
  • Premature order releases and uncontrolled updates
  • Planned values reused as actuals during confirmation
  • Irreversible service closure executed without readiness checks
  • Missing audit trail across intake, order, and confirmation stages

The net result: slower cycle times, higher operational risk, weaker first-time-right rates, and financial data that Finance and Analytics cannot fully trust.

What service teams need is not another chatbot — it is a governed, conversational execution layer that respects SAP's business rules at every step of the cycle.

The Three Service Management Agents:

Three SAP Joule Agents, one governed lifecycle.

Figure 1 — End-to-end Service Management cycle governed by the three SAP Joule Agents: Service Intake Assistant, Service Order Assistant, and Service Confirmation Assistant. Every stage follows the same pattern — natural language in, governed validation, backend-confirmed outcome.

1. Service Intake Assistant:

The starting point of the cycle. This agent delivers a governed, AI-assisted intake experience for Service Requests and Service Quotations. It resolves Business Partners from SAP master data, validates mandatory intake information in a single consolidated step, and creates or updates service documents only after successful SAP backend execution — ensuring first-time-right intake and eliminating incomplete service documents at the source.

Because the Service Intake Assistant is the entry point to the entire service cycle, the following walkthrough illustrates its behavior in depth. The same conversational, consolidated-validation, backend-confirmed pattern is then applied consistently by the Service Order Assistant and the Service Confirmation Assistant across the rest of the lifecycle.

 

 

Screenshot 1 — Launching the Service Intake Assistant inside SAP Joule for a natural-language conversation with a service user.

The interaction begins with a simple natural-language request from the service user. The agent then resolves the Business Partner directly against SAP master data and retrieves the partner's existing service documents before creating anything new.

Screenshot 2 — The Service Intake Assistant resolves the Business Partner against SAP master data and retrieves all existing Service Requests for the partner before any new document is created.

Once the Business Partner is confirmed, the agent gathers every mandatory field in a single consolidated interaction rather than a long back-and-forth. If anything is missing, ambiguous, or blocking, the agent communicates it transparently along with what has already been collected — nothing is assumed, nothing is fabricated.

 

Screenshot 3 — Consolidated single-step validation in action: the agent displays every field already available and transparently flags the single missing mandatory input before proceeding to backend execution.

Only when all validations pass does the agent invoke the corresponding SAP backend operation. Success is confirmed only after SAP S/4HANA returns a valid document number — never before.

 

 

Screenshot 4 — Backend-confirmed Service Request creation in SAP S/4HANA Service Management — the agent confirms success only after the SAP backend returns a valid document number.

The same governed pattern applies to Service Quotations, which the agent creates against the confirmed Business Partner with full pricing, quantity, validity, and status returned directly from SAP.

 

Screenshot 5 — Backend-confirmed Service Quotation creation for the same Business Partner, with pricing, quantity, validity, and status returned directly from SAP S/4HANA.

Updates to existing service documents follow the same discipline. The agent applies the change only after backend confirmation and presents a clear before-and-after view so that the update is auditable.

 

Screenshot 6 — Controlled update of an existing Service Quotation, with the agent showing the previous and new values only after backend confirmation of the update. 

2. Service Order Assistant:

The execution governance layer of the cycle. This agent controls Service Order creation, updates, and release. It ensures that Service Orders are created only from valid upstream Service Quotation references, enforces consolidated dependency validation, and executes release only when SAP backend conditions are met — eliminating unauthorized or orphan Service Orders and preventing premature releases.

The following walkthrough illustrates the Service Order lifecycle end-to-end: retrieve, create, update, and release.

 

Screenshot 7 — Launching the Service Order Assistant inside SAP Joule, which supports the complete Service Order lifecycle: retrieve, create, update, and release.

Before any Service Order is created, the agent enforces its most important governance rule — the presence of a valid upstream Service Quotation reference. Only after the Quotation is confirmed does the agent request the mandatory fields for Service Order creation.

 

Screenshot 8 — Upstream dependency enforcement: the Service Order Assistant validates the referenced Service Quotation before collecting the mandatory fields required for Service Order creation.

When all validations pass, the agent executes the create operation and confirms success only after SAP S/4HANA returns a valid Service Order number, linked back to the originating Quotation for traceability.

 

Screenshot 9 — Backend-confirmed Service Order creation, linked to the upstream Service Quotation. Success is confirmed only after SAP S/4HANA returns a valid Service Order number.

Updates to existing Service Orders follow the same discipline. The agent applies changes only after backend confirmation and presents a clear before-and-after view for auditability.

 

Screenshot 10 — Controlled item-level update of the Service Order, with the agent showing previous and new values only after successful backend confirmation.

Release is treated as a distinct governance event. Before any release is executed, the agent runs a full Release Readiness Assessment — verifying every prerequisite and reporting blockers transparently. Release is permitted only when the assessment returns clean.

 

Screenshot 11 — Release Readiness Assessment: the Service Order Assistant verifies every release prerequisite (existence, status, upstream reference, organizational data, service details) and reports "no blockers detected" before any release is executed.

Once released, the Service Order is officially available for work scheduling and execution — which is the natural handoff cue to the Service Confirmation Assistant, the third agent in the cycle.

 

Screenshot 12 — Backend-confirmed release of the Service Order in SAP S/4HANA. The agent confirms release status, pricing document creation, and execution readiness only after successful backend validation — completing the governed handoff into service execution.

3. Service Confirmation Assistant:

The closure layer of the cycle. This agent governs actuals-based Service Confirmation creation, updates, and final completion. It ensures that confirmations are recorded only against valid, released Service Orders, enforces a clear separation between planned and actual execution data, and performs final completion only after SAP backend confirmation — treating closure as the irreversible business action it is.

The following walkthrough illustrates the complete Service Confirmation lifecycle: retrieve, create, update, and complete.

 

Screenshot 13 — Launching the Service Confirmation Assistant inside SAP Joule. The agent supports the complete Service Confirmation lifecycle: retrieve, create, update, and — as an explicitly irreversible action — complete.

Before any confirmation is created, the agent verifies the upstream Service Order's release status and reports whether confirmations already exist. This is the discipline that keeps actuals aligned to real, released work.

 

Screenshot 14 — Before any confirmation is created, the agent retrieves the upstream Service Order's release status and reports the existing confirmation state — enforcing the rule that actuals can only be recorded against valid, released Service Orders.

Once readiness is confirmed, the agent gathers every mandatory field in a single consolidated request — the same design pattern applied by the Intake and Order Assistants.

 

Screenshot 15 — Consolidated mandatory field collection for Service Confirmation creation — the same single-step validation pattern applied by every agent in the Service Management set.

The agent then executes the create operation and confirms success only after SAP S/4HANA returns a valid Service Confirmation ID, linked back to the released Service Order for full traceability.

 

Screenshot 16 — Backend-confirmed Service Confirmation creation in SAP S/4HANA, linked back to the released Service Order. Success is confirmed only after the SAP backend returns a valid Service Confirmation ID.

Updates continue to follow the same auditable discipline as the previous agents in the cycle — with a clean before-and-after view after every backend-confirmed change.

 

Screenshot 17 — Controlled update of an existing Service Confirmation, with the agent presenting previous and new values only after backend confirmation — the same auditability pattern applied across the cycle.

Finally, completion is treated as the irreversible business action that it is. The agent captures actual service duration and start time, performs final completion only after SAP backend confirmation, and explicitly communicates that the confirmation is finalized and cannot be undone.

Screenshot 18 — Irreversible Service Confirmation completion in SAP S/4HANA. The agent records actual service duration and start time, finalizes the confirmation as an audit-ready, non-reversible business action, and closes the end-to-end service cycle that began with the Service Intake Assistant.

How the Agents Work Together:

The Service Intake walkthrough above illustrated a design pattern that all three agents share: natural language in, governed SAP execution out.

The Service Order Assistant applies the same discipline to Service Order creation, updates, and release — refusing to create orders without a valid upstream Service Quotation reference, enforcing consolidated dependency validation, and releasing only when SAP backend conditions are met.

The Service Confirmation Assistant closes the loop with the same rigor — recording actual only against released Service Orders, separating planned and actual data cleanly, and performing final closure only after SAP backend confirmation.

The result is a service lifecycle in which no stage can be skipped, and no stage can be executed without the previous one being genuinely valid — exactly what enterprise-grade service governance requires.

 

Figure 2 — Shared design pattern across all three agents: user intent expressed in natural language, consolidated context and validation, governed SAP backend execution, and confirmed outcome that hands off cleanly to the next stage of the service lifecycle.

Solution Architecture:

All three agents are built entirely on SAP's AI and application stack.

Figure 3 — Technical realization on SAP Business Technology Platform: SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Build Process Automation, SAP Joule Studio, SAP AI Core, and SAP Cloud Identity Services deployed in a multi-cloud subaccount, connected to on-premises SAP S/4HANA or SAP ECC through Cloud Connector via OData/REST and HTTPS.

Architecture Components:

  • SAP Joule — Conversational interaction layer for service users.
  • SAP Joule Studio — Design, configure, and orchestrate each agent, its skills, guardrails, and prompts.
  • SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) — Runtime, identity, and extensibility foundation shared across the agent set.
  • Generative AI Hub in SAP AI Core — Governed access to large language models, prompt orchestration, and AI governance.
  • SAP S/4HANA Service Management — System of record for Business Partners, Service Requests, Service Quotations, Service Orders, and Service Confirmations.

Business Value:

Adopting the three Service Management agents together delivers value that no single-point automation can match:

  • First-time-right service intake — Governed data collection and Business Partner resolution eliminate incomplete Service Requests and Quotations at the source.
  • Controlled, traceable execution — Every Service Order is created and released from a valid upstream reference, preventing unauthorized or orphan orders.
  • Accurate, irreversible closure — Confirmations capture real actuals against released orders, protecting billing accuracy and audit readiness.
  • Faster cycle times — Removing manual navigation and fragmented validation shortens intake, execution, and closure across the lifecycle.
  • Trustworthy service data — Finance and Analytics receive service data that reflects real backend execution, not optimistic UI states.
  • Reduced operational risk — Governance is enforced by design across every stage, not left to individual user discipline.

Example Use Cases:

  • A Service Desk Operator captures a customer request and creates a Service Request through a natural-language conversation, without navigating any Fiori app.
  • A Service Order Coordinator converts an approved Service Quotation into a controlled Service Order and releases it once all dependencies are satisfied.
  • A Field Service Technician records actual time and effort against a released Service Order and closes the service as a final, irreversible action.
  • A Customer Service Manager retrieves and reviews open Service Quotations, Service Orders, or Service Confirmations for a specific Business Partner through a single conversational query.
  • An Operations Lead uses the same conversational surface across all three agents to move a service case cleanly from request to closure.

Lessons Learned Building an End-to-End Agent Set:

Building not one but three interlinked SAP Joule Agents surfaced lessons that are directly relevant to anyone building on SAP Joule Studio and the Generative AI Hub.

  1. Design for the Cycle, Not for the Agent

The single biggest architectural decision was to design the three agents as a coherent set rather than three isolated agents. Handoffs between intake, order, and confirmation are where enterprise value is created — and where governance is most easily lost.

  1. Governance Is Non-Negotiable at Every Stage

Every stage of the service cycle has its own point at which mistakes become expensive. The agents therefore enforce validation, upstream references, and backend confirmation at every stage, not just at the point of creation.

  1. Consolidated Validation Beats Iterative Prompting

Users abandon agents that ask for information one field at a time. Collecting and validating in a single consolidated step turned out to be one of the highest-impact UX decisions across all three agents.

  1. Never Confirm Before the Backend Confirms

Optimistic confirmations erode trust immediately. Every agent in the set returns success only when SAP S/4HANA returns a valid outcome — even when that makes the conversation slightly longer.

  1. Actuals Deserve the Same Rigor as Creation

Service Confirmations are the stage where planned and actual data are most often confused. Treating actuals capture and final closure with the same rigor as intake and order creation was essential to protect billing accuracy and audit readiness.

  1. A Shared Design Language Accelerates Everything

Because all three agents follow the same natural-language-in, backend-confirmed-out pattern, users learn one interaction model and reuse it across the entire cycle — which is a significant adoption advantage.

Conclusion:

The Service Intake Assistant, Service Order Assistant, and Service Confirmation Assistant together demonstrate how SAP Business AI, SAP Joule, SAP Joule Studio, and the SAP Generative AI Hub can be used to govern an entire enterprise business cycle — not just automate a single step.

The three agents show that agentic AI on SAP does not have to trade off governance for productivity. When designed as a coherent set, SAP Joule Agents can deliver faster execution, higher data quality, controlled handoffs, and audit-ready outcomes across the full service cycle.

As enterprises accelerate their SAP Business AI journeys, an end-to-end agent set like this represents a practical, production-ready path to governed, conversational service execution.

If you are exploring SAP Joule Studio or building your first SAP Joule Agent, I would be happy to hear how you are approaching governance, consolidated validation, and backend confirmation in your own designs.

Please share your thoughts in the comments below.

About the Author:

The author is a Lead Architect working on SAP Business AI, SAP Joule, and SAP BTP, focused on the design and delivery of governed SAP Joule Agents for enterprise business processes on SAP S/4HANA.

entry.title: Re: CDS view to get the PO Workflow data
entry.content: Released CDS VIEWS I_WorkflowStatusDetails - Top level work item status I_WorkflowStatusOverview - Current work item READY in inbox I_WorkflowRecipients - Shows the user to approve the work item I_WorkflowTaskExternalStatus - all availabel statuses I_WorkflowExternalStatus - all available statuses I_WorkflowExternalStatusText - All status texts in each language None of these views expose the child/step-level work item data (the individual READY and PLANNED rows) that you can see in SWWWIHEAD table. There is no standard released CDS view that surfaces the individual step-level work item statuses (e.g., READY, PLANNED) stored in that table https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-q-a/whitelisted-cds-view-to-get-approval-details-of-purchase-requisition/qaq-p/12335509 https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-q-a/custom-report-to-provide-approval-dates-for-each-step-of-workflow/qaq-p/12324435

entry.title: Currency Conversion in SAP Datasphere: Understanding TCUR* Tables and Practical Implementation
entry.content:
Currency Conversion in SAP Datasphere

 

GenAI Assisted Content: This article was created using a combination of SAP Help documentation, practical SAP Datasphere implementation experience, and Generative AI tools for research, structuring, and drafting. All technical details, examples, validations, and conclusions have been reviewed and verified by the author.

 

Introduction:

During financial and management reporting, organizations often need to consolidate transactions originating from multiple currencies into a single reporting currency. While SAP Datasphere provides built-in currency conversion capabilities, the accuracy of the converted results depends on several SAP standard currency management tables and configuration settings working together.

An exchange rate alone is not sufficient to determine the final converted amount. SAP uses multiple tables to manage exchange rates, conversion factors, quotation methods, validity dates, decimal precision, and other conversion rules. Understanding these components is essential for ensuring accurate reporting and reconciliation.

This blog explores the SAP standard TCUR* framework, explains the role of key currency tables, demonstrates currency conversion implementation in SAP Datasphere, and provides practical validation examples that can help consultants troubleshoot and verify conversion results.

 

Why I Decided to Explore This Topic

During a recent SAP Datasphere project, I was responsible for validating currency conversion results against SAP S/4HANA. Although Datasphere provided the conversion functionality out of the box, I noticed that small differences in configuration could produce unexpected results during reconciliation.

As I started investigating these differences, I realized that currency conversion involved much more than maintaining exchange rates. Several configuration settings and dependencies influenced the final result, making troubleshooting more complex than I initially expected.

This prompted me to spend additional time exploring the underlying SAP currency conversion framework and understanding how Datasphere interprets the TCUR* tables. The insights gathered during that investigation became the foundation for this blog, and I hope they help fellow consultants troubleshoot currency conversion scenarios more efficiently.

Business Use Case:

Consider a global organization operating across multiple countries.

For example:

  • Sales transactions are posted in CAD
  • Procurement transactions are posted in EUR
  • Headquarters requires consolidated reporting in USD

Without a consistent currency conversion process, financial reports become difficult to compare and may produce misleading results.

SAP Datasphere provides built-in currency conversion capabilities, but accurate results depend on the correct interpretation of exchange rates, conversion factors, quotation methods, decimal settings, and exchange rate dates.

Reader Note: This blog is divided into two sections:

Section 1 – SAP Currency Conversion Framework: Explains the SAP standard currency tables (TCURR, TCURF, TCURN, TCURX, etc.) and the conversion logic behind currency translation.

Section 2 – Currency Conversion in SAP Datasphere: Demonstrates the implementation, configuration, validation, and troubleshooting steps in SAP Datasphere.

If you are already familiar with the SAP currency tables, feel free to jump directly to Section 2.

 

Section 1: SAP Currency Conversion Framework

The following SAP standard currency tables (or corresponding currency conversion views) need to be available in Datasphere for currency conversion.

1. TCURC (Currency Codes):

This table contains the list of all valid currencies. All other currency-related tables depend on this table.

 

2. TCURV (Exchange Rate Types for Currency Translation):

  • This table defines exchange rate types (e.g., M, B, G, etc.). The most commonly used exchange rate type is M, which is typically used for average rate-based currency conversion.
  • It determines which exchange rate should be used during conversion.

 

 

3. TCURR (Exchange Rates):

This table stores the actual exchange rate (UKURS) between two currencies for a given ‘Valid From’ date (GDATU).

Note: The field GDATU has both internal and external formats.
You can view this by enabling “Apply Conversion Exit” in the S/4 system.

Navigation:
SE11 → TCURR → Display → Contents → Execute → Settings → User Parameters

Exchange rates maintained in transaction OB08 are stored in TCURR, along with their corresponding GDATU (Valid From Date) values.

Below example (OB08 T code screenshot) shows exchange rate between JPY(Japanese Yen) to VEB (Venezuelan Bolivar (Old)).

 

 4. TCURF (Conversion Factors):

  • This table stores the ratio and conversion factors used for currency translations.
  • It works together with TCURR to define how many units of “From” currency equal how many units of “To” currency.

 

 

The FFACT (Ratio From) and TFACT (Ratio To) fields are available in both TCURR and TCURF.

Relationship between TCURR and TCURF

 

TCURR

TCURF

Purpose

Stores the rate value Exchange Rate (UKURS) for specific currency pairs

Stores the ratio/conversion factors visible in Transaction OB08

Field

Although FFACT and TFACT fields are available in TCURR, the conversion factors used for currency conversion are typically derived from TCURF, which stores the maintained ratio information used by the conversion logic.

These fields are authoritative. The system reads them to determine the multiplier/divisor

Converted Amount = (Amount × UKURS × FFACT) / TFACT

Note: The exact conversion logic ultimately depends on the quotation method (Direct/Indirect) and conversion settings maintained in SAP.

5. TCURN (Quotations):

It controls how the exchange rate is interpreted during conversion. It defines whether the system treats the maintained rate as Direct or Indirect quotation.

Direct Quotation -> The maintained exchange rate represents the value of one unit of source currency in target currency. Exchange rate is applied using multiplication.

Indirect Quotation -> The maintained exchange rate represents the inverse relationship. Exchange rate is applied using division.

Important: TCURN does not store the rate, it controls how the maintained rate is applied during conversion.

Let’s continue with previous example (OB08 screenshot).

Based on the maintained ratio:

  • JPY = 610 VEB

Therefore:

1 JPY = 6.1 VEB

 

Direct Quotation: How many units of the target currency equal 1 unit of the base currency

Example: 1 JPY = 6.10 VEB (Multiply during conversion)

Indirect Quotation: How many units of the base currency equal 1 unit of the target currency.

Example: 1 VEB = 0.16393 JPY (Divide during conversion)

Conclusion: Direct and indirect quotation do not change the maintained exchange rate value itself. They influence how the rate is interpreted and applied during conversion, for example through multiplication or division, based on the quotation indicator.

6. TCURX (Decimal Places in Currencies):

This table defines the number of decimal places maintained for each currency and is used during rounding and display of converted currency amounts.

 

7. TCURT (Currency Codes: Texts):

This table contains language-dependent descriptions of currency codes.

 

Currency Key

Description

USD

US Dollar

EUR

Euro

JPY

Japanese Yen

INR

Indian Rupee

 

Although TCURT is not involved in currency conversion calculations, it can be used in SAP Datasphere to display meaningful currency descriptions instead of currency keys in reports, stories, and analytical models.

 

The following sequence illustrates how SAP internally processes currency conversion using the TCUR tables.

Datasphere Conversion Flow

Input Data (Currency Field)

TCURC → Validate currency

TCURV → Determine rate type

TCURR → Get exchange rate (Stores Rate)

TCURF → Apply conversion factors (Stores Scaling)

TCURN → Apply direction (Multiply/Divide) (Controls Direction)

TCURX → Apply rounding/decimals (Controls Decimals)

 

Section 2: Currency Conversion in SAP Datasphere 

Currency Conversion in Datasphere (Using a Graphical View):

In my example: I want to convert ACDOCA-TSL (Amount in Balance Transaction Currency) to ‘USD’ based on the ACDOCA-BUDAT (Posting Date).

 

Create a simple fact view using the table, where you want to apply currency conversion.

Apply Filters, if you want to restrict the model for specific data.

 

Now create a formula using ‘Currency Conversion Column’ to convert the value to desired currency.

 

Maintain below settings for the newly created ‘Currency Conversion Column’.

The screenshot above shows the currency conversion configuration used in this example. The following sections explain each setting in detail and describe its impact on the final conversion result.

 

Currency Properties – Steps:
There are 4 steps available. Shift, Convert, Round, Shift Back

Which step is required when?

Step

Required?

When to Enable

Reason / Behavior

Shift

Conditional

If FFACT ≠ 1

Enable when source-side scaling/factors are relevant. It matters, in scenarios such as 100 JPY = 1 VEB

Convert

Yes

Always

Core conversion logic using exchange rate (TCURR)

Round

Optional

For reporting/output

Applies currency decimal rules (TCURX)

Shift Back

Conditional

If TFACT ≠ 1

Enable when target-side scaling/factors are relevant. It matters in scenarios such as 1 BRL = 1000 COP.

 

In many SAP systems, FFACT and TFACT are maintained as 1:1. In such cases, enabling only Convert (and optionally Round) will typically produce the same result. Shift and Shift Back become important when conversion ratios other than 1:1 are maintained in TCURF.

  • If FFACT = TFACT = 1 → Only Convert is required
  • If FFACT or TFACT is not equal to 1 → Shift and Shift Back are critical
  • FFACT > 1 → Enable Shift
  • TFACT > 1 → Enable Shift Back

 

Provide source measure related currency field name in ‘Source Currency’.

In this example, I am giving Target Currency as ‘USD’, but based on requirement, you can define ‘Input Parameter’ and give that as ‘Target Currency’.

 

Reference Date: Specify the date that should be used for currency conversion. For each record, Datasphere uses the reference date (for example, the posting date) to identify the valid exchange rate maintained in TCURR and selects the exchange rate record whose GDATU (Valid From Date) is the most recent date that is valid for the conversion date.

 

Currency Properties – Conversion Type: The appropriate Conversion Type should be confirmed with the business or functional consultant.

Currency Properties – Error Handling: There are 3 options available. Fail on Error, Set to Null, Keep Unconverted.

 

Error Handling Option

What Happens on Error

Result in Output

When to Use

Risk Level

Fail on Error

Stops execution immediately

No output (error raised)

Financial reporting, validation, production checks

Safe

Set to Null

Continues execution

Value becomes NULL

Partial data scenarios

Moderate

Keep Unconverted

Skips conversion

Original source amount is retained

Mixed currency data, fallback scenarios

Risky

 

Note: ‘Keep Unconverted’ can silently produce incorrect financial reports

 

There are two different options available in

Advanced Properties – Lookup: Regular and Reverse

Property

Regular Lookup

Reverse Lookup

Concept

Performs a standard forward conversion using the exchange rate directly defined in the source and target currency pair.

Performs a conversion by swapping (reversing) the input units. It locates a rate where the source and target currencies are flipped in the system.

Use Case

Used when the exchange rate is directly maintained in the system (Example:  converting USD to EUR directly).

Used when a direct exchange rate from Source to Target is not maintained, but a direct rate for the opposite direction exists (Example: needing to convert to EUR, but only EUR to USD rates are maintained.)

Exchange Rate Type

Uses the direct Exchange Rate Type (Example: M for Average Rate) as stored.

Calculates the reciprocal of the Exchange Rate Type maintained in the system.

 

  • If same direction → Regular
  • If reverse direction → Reverse

 

There are 2 options in ‘Advance Properties’-‘Accuracy’: Compatibility and Highest

 

Option

What it does

Result Behavior

When to Use

Example

Compatibility

Matches SAP (S/4 / BW) currency conversion logic exactly

Same rounding, same precision as SAP

When you need Datasphere = SAP output

Base Amount: 10,000,000
Exchange Rate: 1.1215437892
Result: 11,215,400.00 USD

Here the exchange rate is rounded off early to match the data type limits of standard ERP tables (typically 5 decimal places).

Highest

Uses maximum technical precision (no SAP-style truncation)

More decimals, more accurate mathematically

When precision matters more than SAP match

Base Amount: 10,000,000
Exchange Rate: 1.1215437892
Result: 11,215,437.89 USD

 

In the Advanced Properties, there are different options mentioned below.

 

There are 2 options in ‘Advance Properties’-‘Date Format’: Auto Detect, Normal and Inverted.

Choosing the wrong date format may not always raise an error, but it can cause Datasphere to interpret the rate validity date incorrectly and select an unexpected exchange rate.

Option

When to Use

Typical Data Source

What Happens

Risk if Wrong

Auto Detect

When you are unsure how the exchange rate date is stored

External files (CSV, Excel), multiple systems

System auto-identifies date format

May misinterpret ambiguous dates

Normal

When date is in DD-MM-YYYY or MM-DD-YYYY, or similar human-readable formats

External systems, regional files (Europe/India formats)

Treats date as standard calendar format

Swaps day and month interpretation, picking wrong exchange rate

Inverted

When the exchange rate date is stored in SAP inverted/internal format, such as GDATU in TCUR* tables

SAP S/4, BW, Datasphere models

Treats the date as SAP internal/inverted date format.

Safe for SAP data, but completely wrong for DD-MM-YYYY input

 

As shown in the screenshot above, these entities (Precisions Entity, Configuration Entity, Prefactors Entity, Rates Entity, Notations Entity, and Currency Code Entity) should be mapped to the corresponding TCUR* tables or views used for currency conversion.

Note: If you want to use views provided by SAP, you have to import them. Below link helps in import process.

Enabling Currency Conversion with TCUR* Tables and Views | SAP Help Portal

At this point, the currency conversion configuration is complete, and the converted amount can be generated using the Currency Conversion Column.

 

To simplify the explanation, this example converts CAD to USD using a calculated column. In productive scenarios, the target currency is often determined dynamically through input parameters or reporting requirements, so this additional calculated column may not be required.

 

Under ‘Formula’ node create a ‘Calculated Column’ (Optional)

 

Once all settings are maintained, deploy the view and do data preview.

 

Manual Data Validation:

OB08 T- Code screenshot

 

 

 

This is Direct Quotation

 

Converted_Value_of_TSL = (2200 * 0.73934 * 1) / 1 = 1626.548 USD

Converted value is verified and correct.

Note: In the ‘Currency Properties’-‘Steps’, if we enable Round, the result will show 1626.55 (Based on decimals maintained against the target currency in TCURX table).   The above formula represents the direct quotation scenario used in this example. If indirect quotation is maintained, the conversion logic applies the rate direction accordingly based on the quotation setting.

 

When Is Currency Conversion Needed?

Currency conversion is commonly required when:

  • Multiple countries operate in different local currencies
  • Corporate reporting requires a common group currency
  • Management dashboards display consolidated financial results
  • SAP and non-SAP systems provide transactional data in mixed currencies
  • Historical transactions must be converted using exchange rates valid on specific dates

Without a standardized conversion process, financial comparisons across entities, countries, or reporting periods can become unreliable.

Common Pitfalls:

During this implementation, I identified several areas that frequently cause incorrect conversion results:

  • Using the wrong quotation type (Direct vs Indirect) can cause multiplication instead of division.
  • Incorrect Date Format settings may select the wrong exchange rate date.
  • Ignoring FFACT and TFACT values can lead to incorrect conversions when ratios are not maintained as 1:1.
  • Using "Keep Unconverted" as an error handling option can result in misleading financial reports because source amounts remain unchanged.

 

Lessons Learned:

During this exercise, I observed the following:

  • Currency conversion depends on multiple SAP standard tables, not just TCURR.
  • TCURF conversion factors can be just as important as the exchange rate itself.
  • TCURN controls how the maintained rate is interpreted during conversion.
  • Date interpretation settings can significantly impact conversion results.
  • Datasphere results should always be validated against SAP standard tables.
  • Compatibility mode is generally preferred when reconciliation with SAP S/4HANA is required.

 

Conclusion:

Although SAP Datasphere provides built-in currency conversion capabilities, achieving accurate results requires a clear understanding of the underlying SAP currency conversion framework and configuration settings.

For SAP Datasphere consultants, knowledge of the TCUR* tables can significantly simplify validation, reconciliation, and troubleshooting activities when working with multi-currency reporting scenarios.

I hope this blog serves as a practical reference for consultants implementing currency conversion in SAP Datasphere and helps bridge the gap between SAP standard configuration and analytical reporting requirements.

entry.title: Re: SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud | Business Roles for User Ma...
entry.content:
Hi QuocDT5,

 

Thanks for reaching out via SAP Community.

Regarding to your requirements, for User Management, you may need to use the application "Maintain Business Users", and for Role Management, you may need to use the application "Maintain Business Roles".

For app "Maintain Business Users", the required business catalog is 'SAP_CORE_BC_IAM_UM',  you can refer to it in Fiori Library: https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#/detail/Apps('F1303')/S37

And for app "Maintain Business Roles", the required business catalog is 'SAP_CORE_BC_IAM_RM', please refer to https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#/detail/Apps('F1492')/S37

Generally, we don't separate the user management and role management, as while maintaining business users, you may need to maintain the business roles assigned to the user. And while maintaining business roles, you may need to maintain the users that assigned with the role. But theoretically, it should be possible.

 

Best regards,
Jannie
SAP Support

entry.title: SAP Business Network for Logistics 2607 – What’s New?
entry.content:
The July 2026 release of SAP Business Network for Logistics delivers a set of targeted enhancements across both SAP Business Network Freight Collaboration and SAP Business Network Global Track and Trace. This release focuses on improving data consistency, operational usability, tracking transparency, and process flexibility. As with previous releases, the innovations are designed to reduce manual effort, improve data quality, and strengthen collaboration across logistics networks.

Below, we highlight the most important innovations. Make sure to read to the end to learn about an upcoming webinar for shippers!

 

Cross SAP Business Network Freight Collaboration and SAP Business Network Global Track and Trace

New Google Cloud data center available in the US

SAP Business Network for Logistics is now also available on Google Cloud in the United States, expanding hosting options for customers and supporting additional regional deployment requirements.

Business benefit: Customers gain greater flexibility in selecting deployment regions while benefiting from SAP’s continued expansion of cloud infrastructure options.

Data contributors can now indicate when tracking initialization fails

A new event type, TRACK_INIT_ERROR, has been introduced. Data contributors can use this event to indicate that tracking initialization could not be started successfully utilizing ‘Consumer API for Order Tracking (GTT)’ and ‘Consumer API for Order Tracking (FC)’, ‘Provider API for Order Events (GTT)’ and ‘Provider API for Order Events (FC)’.

Business benefit: Increased transparency around tracking failures

 

SAP Business Network Freight Collaboration

Freight order updates now include all carrier reference documents after confirmation

To improve data consistency, freight order updates that are sent after a carrier confirmation now contain the complete set of carrier reference documents, including both existing and newly added references. This ensures that all parties work with a consistent and up-to-date document set throughout the transportation process.

Business benefit: Improved synchronization of freight order information reduces the risk of missing reference data and helps ensure consistent execution across connected systems.

Dialogue harmonization in the Manage Gate Operations app

To simplify the user experience, SAP has harmonized the check-in and check-out dialogs in the ‘Manage Gate Operations’ application.

For check-in processing, the ‘Reason Codes’ dropdown has been removed. Similarly, during check-out processing, the reason code selection has been removed and replaced with a more flexible ‘Remarks’ field.

 

 Business benefit: A cleaner and more intuitive user interface reduces complexity for gate operators and supports more consistent data capture.

Enhanced appointment identification logic for dock appointments

The appointment identification logic for dock appointment APIs has been enhanced to improve data integrity and lifecycle management.

When an appointment ID is provided, the system now validates that the appointment exists. If the appointment cannot be found, an error is returned instead of falling back to alternative search logic. When no appointment ID is supplied, the system uses the external appointment ID to either update an existing appointment or create a new one if no match is found.

Business benefit: This enhancement helps prevent duplicate appointments, improves error handling, and strengthens overall appointment data quality.

Greater flexibility for switching between invoicing and self-billing

Shippers can now change the self-billing indicator for existing freight orders through the ‘Define Business Partners’ application in SAP Transportation Management.

This enhancement addresses onboarding and configuration scenarios where organizations initially selected the wrong billing approach and only discovered the issue after productive operations had started. The billing process can now be changed from invoicing to self-billing—or vice versa—provided there are no active follow-on documents, open disputes, or open invoices.

Business benefit: Customers gain greater operational flexibility and can correct billing-process configuration issues without requiring manual data corrections.

 

SAP Business Network Global Track and Trace

Data contributors can now indicate when tracking initialization fails

Above, you learned that a new event type, TRACK_INIT_ERROR, has been introduced and can be shared via API. The event can be configured in the ‘Manage Models’ application by adding the new event type under shipment model field reasonCode and assigning the event as admissible unplanned event to TU, FU, ….

The event is displayed in the event tables of the SAP Business Network Global Track and Trace Fulfillment Tracking applications, allowing users to identify tracking startup issues more quickly.

Business benefit: Increased transparency around tracking failures allows operations teams to identify and resolve issues sooner, reducing blind spots in shipment visibility processes.

Please note: This event type is not visible in the ‘Manage Freight Execution’ app and is not forwarded to TM.

Reporting Party information available in event tables

Users can now display the Reporting Party directly within the event table of the Fulfillment Tracking apps through personal table settings. Additional reporting party details are available through a dedicated information popup.

Business benefit: Improved transparency helps users quickly identify the source of reported events, supporting faster analysis and issue resolution.

Consistent display of one-time locations

One-time locations are now displayed in the same format as locations maintained in the location master. This enhancement applies across Fulfillment Tracking applications, including worklists, event tables, and tracking timelines.

 

Improved planned technical timestamp calculation

SAP has enhanced the calculation logic for planned technical timestamps by incorporating the default technical tolerance settings more accurately.

Business benefit: Improved timestamp accuracy supports more reliable event evaluation and tracking processes.

 

Announcement of upcoming webinar

We are proud to present a new webinar with the SAP Business Network for Logistics team with a focus on shipper onboarding for freight collaboration.

The content of the webinar will include

  • Learning step-by-step how to create a subaccount in SAP Business Technology Platform, establish trust configurations, and subscribe to the required services to enable the shipper tenant.
  • The session will also show how to access and begin using the shipper environment after setup.
  • By the end, you will understand the prerequisites, including required permissions and account access, and be able to carry out the onboarding and configuration process confidently

There are two different sessions being held for this:

  • Option 1: https://event.on24.com/wcc/r/5383663/FBA434EF32C72894F044B5BAC5D81B64 (August 5, 2026 at 11:00 AM IST)
  • Option 2: https://event.on24.com/wcc/r/5383668/D81B0391039479A6400D6F37FEFA0B79 (August 5, 2026 at 11:00 AM EDT)

 

 -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The above summarizes the key innovations for 2607. We hope you enjoy the new capabilities. Thanks for reading the blog post and stay tuned! 

A full overview of the 2607 release can be found under the following link.

To learn more about SAP Business Network for Logistics visit SAP - Business Network for Logistics.

For further questions and feedback related to the blog, please check the Q&A area and feel free to post your questions in the link with the tag SAP Business Network for Logistics.

Business benefit: Consistent location presentation improves readability and creates a more uniform user experience across tracking applications.

entry.title: Re: Query about notification reminder to send for outstan...
entry.content:
Dear @MaiYel,

Yes but the available solution depends on what is meant by outstanding or updated purchase orders?

For purchase orders with overdue or outstanding deliveries, Public Cloud supports purchase order dunning reminders. For purchase orders created using Create Purchase Order – Advanced, you can maintain the 1st, 2nd and 3rd reminder or expediter intervals in the Delivery tab. For example the intervals can be set to 5, 10 and 15 days after the expected or supplier-confirmed delivery date

The Schedule Dunning Reminder Output for Purchase Orders app can run as a recurring background job and generate the corresponding reminder outputs. The reminders can also be emailed to the supplier when Output Parameter Determination is configured with the relevant output type form template MM_PUR_PO_DUNNING_REMINDER and email template MM_PUR_PO_DUNNING_TEMPLATE. Besides the background job you can also generate and send the reminder manually for selected purchase orders using the app Dunning Reminder on Purchase Order – Advanced

However the standard solution provides three reminder or expediter dates. It does not provide an unlimited rule that continues sending a new reminder every five days indefinitely

For purchase orders pending internal approval a separate reminder can be configured in Manage Workflows for Purchase Orders (F2872). A deadline can be defined after five calendar days or five workdays with the action Send mail notification and a custom copy of the predelivered template MMPUR_PO_WFL_DEADLINE_EMAIL

An updated purchase order does not automatically generate a reminder simply because it was changed. If the change is configured to restart the flexible workflow through configuration activity 103345 – Manage Conditions to Restart Flexible Workflow for Purchase Orders or BAdI MMPUR_PO_WORKFLOW_RESTART the restarted approval task follows the deadline notifications configured in the workflow

More details below:

SAP Help – How to Generate Outputs for Purchase Order Dunning Reminders

SAP Help – Manage Workflows for Purchase Orders

SAP Help – How to Enable Email Notifications for Pending Purchase Order Approvals

SAP KBA 3053155 – Restart Purchase Order Workflow Using SSCUI 103345 and BAdI MMPUR_PO_WORKFLOW_RESTART

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Feuerwerk - Klone deine Rekursion: Einheit 2 - Lektion 5
entry.content:
An English version of this lesson is available at: Get Coding with Snap! - Building Up to AI

 

Videolektion "Feuerwerk"

Diese Lektion kombiniert die Konzepte der letzten beiden Lektionen - Rekursion und Klone - im wahrsten Sinne zu einem Feuerwerk. 

Folge den Schritten aus dem Video in Snap!. Die Aufgaben bauen teilweise auf das Projekt aus dem Video auf. 

 

 

 

 

Du bist dran

  • Importiere das Modul “Animation” in einem neuen Projekt und experimentiere mit den Blöcken. Was kannst du mit ihnen machen?  
    • Lasse ein Objekt realistisch springen, indem du es bescheunigst und abbremst.
    • Animiere ein Objekt so, dass es fällt und realistisch vom Boden abprallt (du kannst dafür sogar einen rekursiven Block erstellen).

 

  • Versuche die “Herz-Explosion” aus dem Video nachzubauen. Mache sie interssanter:
    • setze Grafikeffekte ein
    • wähle die Größe der Klone zufällig
    • erstelle verschiedene Kostüme
    • füge einen Hintergrund hinzu
    • werde kreativ!

  • Hier findest du die Aufgaben auch zum Herunterladen:

    • als PDF: https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/6ihs5rZ?rc=10&doi=SAP1327937 
    • als editierbare docx Version:  https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/GGT4sfX?rc=10&doi=SAP1327976 

    Lösungsvorschläge für Einheit 2 - Lektion 4 findest du in dieser Collection.

 

 

Zusammenfassung - das hast du diese Lektion gelernt

Bausteine dieser Lektion: 

Lässt das Objekt, das im ersten Eingabefeld ausgewählt ist, die Blöcke im zweiten Eingabefeld - dem Ring - ausführen. 
Kann auch in Verbindung mit dem neuer Klon Block verwendet werden, um mit nur einem Block eine Klon zu erzeugen und seine Reaktion zu triggern.

Berichtet einen neuen Klon des Objekts, das den Block ausführt. Kann zum Beispiel als Eingabe für den lasse Block verwendet werden, um einen Klon zu erzeugen und seine Reaktion im selben Skript direkt zu triggern. 

Startet einen neuen Prozess (Thread), der die Blöcke im Ring ausführt.
Die Blöcke im Ring laufen parallel zu den Blöcken, die nach dem starte Block kommen ab. 

 

Parallelismus II

In dieser Lektion hast du eine weitere Möglichkeit kennengelernt, Skripte parallel auszuführen, nämlich mit dem starte Block.

Der starte Block besitzt ein Eingabefeld mit einem grauen Ring, das Befehlsblöcke erwartet. Die Befehlsblöcke im grauen Ring werden in einem separaten Prozess (Thread) ausgeführt. Das bedeutet, dass sie parallel zu den Blöcken ablaufen, die auf den starte Block folgen.

Die beiden folgenden Skripte spielen jeweils drei Töne. Das erste Skript spielt sie nacheinander – als Arpeggio –, sodass jeder Ton erst beginnt, nachdem der vorherige beendet ist. Im zweiten Skript starten alle drei Töne gleichzeitig, dadurch entsteht ein Akkord mit der Dauer eines Schlags.

Führt man die drei "spiele Note" Blöcke wie oben aus, führt das zu einem Arpeggio - die Töne werden nacheinander gespielt. Verwendet man die starte-Blöcke wird für jede Note ein neuer Prozess gestartet und ein Akkord entsteht, bei dem die drei Noten parallel gespielt werden.

 

Im Video haben wir den starte Block verwendet, damit alle Herz-Klone gleichzeitig explodieren und ihre Skripte parallel ausführen.

Wenn du den Block starte weglässt, führt jeder einzelne Herz-Klon zunächst sein gesamtes Skript aus. Erst nachdem dieses vollständig beendet ist, wird der nächste Klon erzeugt. 

Wenn die Klone mit dem "starte Block" explodieren, werden die Explosionen parallel und nicht der Reihe nach ausgeführt.

 

 

Und jetzt?

Das wars mit Einheit 2 - Lektion 5: Feuerwerk - Klone deine Rekursion.

  • Zur nächsten Lektion des Kurses geht es hier: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-young-thinkers-program-blog-posts/zum-guten-schluss-der-knaller-einheit-2-video-6/ba-p/14447067 
  • Zur Übersichtsseite von Einheit 2 geht es hier: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-young-thinkers-program-blog-posts/einheit-2-bausteine-f%C3%BCr-syst... 
  • Zur Übersichtsseite des gesamten Kurses geht es hier: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-young-thinkers-program-blog-posts/programmieren-mit-snap-building-u... 
  • Eine Englische Version des Kurses, bei der ein Zertifikat erworben werden kann, findet sich hier: https://learning.sap.com/courses/get-coding-with-snap-building-up-to-ai 

 

Dieses Material ist veröffentlicht unter der CC 4.0 Lizenz durch das Snap! Team bei SAP
